content: MY DEAREST FRIEND,
But, what comfort could I have had, for two whole days, at Deal?
I am sorry to tell you, that dr Baird is so ill, that I am told it is very probable he may never recover.
This place is the devil's, for dreadful colds: and I don't believe I should get well all the winter; for both cough, and bowels, are still very much out of order.
You are now writing your last letter for Deal; so am I, for Merton, from Deal: at least, I hope so; for, if I can help it, I will not return to it.
I have much to do, being the last day on board; but ever, my dearest friend, believe me your truly affectionate
NELSON and BRONTE.
I am literally starving with cold; but my heart is warm.
I suppose I shall dine with Lutwidge: but I am not very desirous of it; for I shall have Sutton, Bedford, and Hardy, with me.
Yesterday, he sold a pair of silver buckles; he would soon ruin poor Charles, who is really a well disposed boy.
I never shall get warm again, I believe.
I cannot feel the pen.
LETTER thirty one.
MY DEAREST EMMA,
It will make it truly uncomfortable; but, I cannot help myself.
I assure you, my dear Emma, that I feel a thorough conviction, that we shall meet again, with honour, riches, and health, and remain together till a good old age.
I look at your and my God's Child's picture; but, till I am sure of remaining here, I cannot bring myself to hang them up.
Be assured, that my attachment, and affectionate regard, is unalterable; nothing can shake it!
And, pray, say so to my dear mrs t when you see her.
We are very comfortable.
mr Elliot is happy, has quite recovered his spirits; he was very low, at Portsmouth.
George Elliot is very well; say so, to Lord Minto.
Hardy is gone into Plymouth, to see our Dutchman safe.
I think, she will turn out a good prize.
If the wind stands, on Tuesday we shall be on the coast of Portugal; and, before next Sunday, in the Mediterranean.
I shall now stop, till I have been on board the Admiral.
Therefore, as you change, make Davison take a direction to Nepean; but, I would not trouble him with too many directions, for fear of embroil.
It blows strong.
So much for the wisdom of my superiors.
I am just embarking in the Amphion; cannot find Admiral Cornwallis.
May God in Heaven bless you! prays your most sincere
NELSON and BRONTE.
Stephens's publication I should like to have.
LETTER thirty two.
[july eighteen o three.
MY DEAREST EMMA,
Our passages have been enormously long.
I am vexed, that she did not mention you!
You will be sorry to hear, that good Sir William did not leave her in such comfortable circumstances as his fortune would have allowed.
He has given it amongst his relations.
But, you think, that she never will, or can.
You will only shew the King and Queen's letters to some few particular friends.
The King is very low; lives, mostly, at Belvidere.
mr Elliot had not seen either him or the Queen, from the seventeenth, the day of his arrival, to the twenty first.
On the next day, he was to be presented.
I have made up my mind, that it is part of the plan of that Corsican Scoundrel, to conquer the kingdom of Naples.

He has marched thirteen thousand men into the kingdom, on the Adriatic side; and he will take possession, with as much shadow of right, of Gaeta and Naples: and, if the poor King remonstrates, or allows us to secure Sicily, he will call it war, and declare a conquest.
The Morea, and Egypt, are likewise in his eye.
Gibbs and Noble are gone to Malta.
I am, you may believe, very anxious to get off Toulon, to join the fleet.
What force they have, I know not; indeed, I am totally ignorant: some say, nine sail of the line; some, seven; some, five.
However, I hope they will come out, and let us settle the matter.
You know, I hate being kept in suspence.
One or two more in about a week.
You will readily believe, how rejoiced I shall be to get one of your dear, excellent letters, that I may know every thing which has passed since my absence.
I sincerely hope, that mr Booth has settled all your accounts.
Never mind, my dear Emma, a few hundred pounds; which is all the rigid gripe of the law, not justice, can wrest from you.
I hope, the minister has done something for you.

Independence is a blessing; and, although I have not yet found out the way to get prize money-what has been taken, has run into our mouths-however, it must turn out very hard, if I cannot get enough to pay off my debts, and that will be no small comfort.
I have not mentioned my Bronte affairs to Acton, as yet; but, if Naples remains much longer, I shall ask the question.
But, I expect nothing from them.
I believe, even Acton wishes himself well, and safely removed.
Certainly, his situation must be heart breaking!
I believe, he saw enough of Naples. He carried his family money; and mr Falconet (Gibbs being absent) will pay mr Greville's pension to Gaetano's family.
Vincenzo has had none paid.
He is very poor; keeps a shop.
Old Antonio was allowed a carline a day; that is, now, not paid.
Francesca has two children living, and another coming.
Pasqual lives with the Duke Montelione; and Joseph, with the old Russian.
So much for Gaetano's news.
We joined, this morning, the fleet.
From The
NELSON,
NOW
EARL NELSON,
TO
LADY HAMILTON.
My Dear Lady,
It was killed yesterday.
How unfortunate it was, we left town as we did!
I had a letter, yesterday morning, from my great and beloved Brother.

A near relation of our's, who has not seen my Lord since his return to England, has offered to take me in his carriage: so, we set out on Sunday afternoon; for we parsons can't go till the Sunday duty is over.
We sleep at Norwich, and hope to be at Yarmouth early on Monday.
I have written to my Brother by this post; so that, if he is likely to have sailed before Monday, he has time to stop us.
I have written you all these particulars; because, I know, you like to know all about us.
We shall return by the following Sunday.
I see, by the papers, the King was better on Tuesday.
mrs Nelson is going out for a day; when she returns, she will write.
We both join in united regards to Sir William; and believe me, your Ladyship's faithful and most obliged and affectionate friend,
Hilborough, march twenty ninth eighteen o one.
My Dear Lady,
I do not much wonder we have no news from the Baltic, considering the state of the wind; and, unless it changes, it may be some time first. Pray God it may be good, when it does arrive.
But, for God's sake, take care of him; and caution our little jewel to be as much upon her guard as she can.
He must be watched with a hawk's eye.
Why should he not be like him?
Is it so very uncommon for such near relations to have some similitude?
My love to deary, Charlotte, and the hereditary Duke of Bronte.
God bless you, my dear Lady; and believe me, your's faithfully,
NELSON.
three.
Hilborough, August twenty three d, eighteen o one.
MY DEAR LADY HAMILTON,
I have written two long letters to my jewel, but I still seem to have more to say.
I hope, Sir William will be able to amuse himself with fishing a little. The weather is too hot for me to come to London, and I can't leave my parish at this time.
I wish you could get a comfortable house near London.
I am perfectly satisfied, that I am in the patent.
I don't mean to say more to my Brother.
Write from Deal, and tell me when you are likely to return to London.
You can't come from thence nearer than London, unless my Brother lands you on the other side of the river Thames, on the Essex or Suffolk coasts.
Compliments to Sir William, and all friends.
NELSON.
Sunday Morning, september sixth, [eighteen o one.]
My Dear Lady,
I wish you was here, and you should not laugh at me for nothing.
I would give you as good as you brought, at any time.
I'll have no Emmas, at present.
But, I hope to God, the present young Horatio will go on as we all wish, and transmit a long race to posterity.
My only fear is, that we shall spoil him among us.
I have not yet heard from him, how he felt himself.
I should have liked to have peeped slyly into his room, and seen how he acted on first receiving the joyful intelligence.
He well knows me; and I leave it to your Ladyship, (my best and truest friend) to say every thing to him, for and from me: it will come best from your lips, and adorned with your eloquence.
MY DEAR LADY HAMILTON,
I hope you will have received my long letter of Sunday's date, by this time.
As I wrote so much on Sunday, and you said-you thought you should leave Deal on Tuesday or Wednesday, I said-I should write no more till you got back to London.
I am glad mrs Nelson is likely to come home soon; but, I hear nothing about your intentions.
mrs Bolton is here for a day, to help my solitary life.
She, and my Father, are to spend the winter in London; and, I am informed, he is to pay half.
mr Edwards is this moment gone, and begs his compliments to you all.
Believe me, your's most faithfully,
NELSON.
Compliments to Parker and Langford.
Dear Lady Hamilton,
The ceremony of electing the new Archbishop takes place on Tuesday morning.
Your's, very faithfully,
NELSON.
